---
title: "User access to Workbench"
description: "A quick overview"
author: "Lisa"
date: "2026-04-13"
format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
editor: source
toc: true
image: "img/"
draft: false
freeze: true
filters:
   - lightbox
lightbox: auto
categories:
  - Posit
---

## Overview

Posit Workbench gives each user their own Linux session with a home directory, running processes, and file-system permissions. This means every Workbench user must have a corresponding system account on the server (or accessible via network services like System Security Services Daemon (SSSD) or Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP)).

:::{.callout-important}
Workbench users require local or networked system accounts regardless of what authentication method you use. Enabling user authentication doesn't also handling user provisioning which needs to be set up separately. 
:::

This is different from Posit Connect and Posit Package Manager, which run content
under a single service account and do not need per-user system accounts.

## How authentication works in Workbench

Workbench uses Linux [Pluggable Authentication Modules (PAM)](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pluggable_authentication_module).

1. **User signs in** — either through a direct login form (PAM) or via your SSO provider (SAML, OpenID Connect, or a reverse proxy).
2. **Workbench maps the user to a system account** — The authenticated username must match a Linux account on the server.
3. **PAM creates a session** — Workbench calls PAM to create a Linux session
   for the matched system account, which sets up the user's environment
   (home directory, groups, and resource limits).

Not all Posit products require local system accounts or PAM. For example, Posit Connect and Shiny Server rely on their own authentication engines and on a single system account for doing their work in most cases, not requiring individualized development environments like the ones offered by Posit Workbench.

::: {.callout-note}
When using SSO, OpenID Connect, or proxied authentication, you need to configure PAM sessions to automatically create system accounts on first sign-in. Otherwise, you must provision local system accounts manually before users can sign in.
:::

## Pros and cons 

Taken from Trevor's excellent slide deck ~July 2025

|Strategy|Pros|Cons|
|---|---|---|
|Manual Linux user accounts|Simple, quick to set up | Doesn’t scale|
|Corporate LDAP/AD|Leverage existing corporate user & group info|Set up outside PWB & difficult for some|
|Corporate SSO OpenID/SAML with SCIM|Leverage existing corporate user info & enterprise IT friendly|No group info (yet) & more setup required in IdP|
|Corporate SSO OpenID/SAML with JIT|Leverage existing corporate user info & easy set up (once you know the settings)|No group info (yet) & less enterprise IT friendly|

## Key concepts: Authentication vs. Sessions vs. Provisioning

Workbench has three components for successful user access: 

| Concept | What it is | 
|---|---|---|
| **Authentication** | Verifying user identity (who is this person?) |
| **PAM Sessions** | Server-side environment setup (home directory, access controls, user permisions) |
| **User Provisioning** | Creating system accounts automatically vs. manually |
---

## Authentication methods

| Method | When to use | Posit Workbench Configuration  |Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Local accounts / PAM Authentication** | Users have local accounts that already exist | [PAM Authentication](pam_authentication.qmd) via `pam_unix` | Direct login form on Workbench |
| **LDAP or Active Directory** | Users have LDAP/AD accounts that already exist | [PAM Authentication](pam_authentication.qmd) via `pam_sss` or `pam_ldap` | Direct login form on Workbench |
| **Kerberos (not SSO)**  | PAM authenticates with Kerberos. | [PAM + Kerberos](kerberos/) via `pam_sss` or `pam_krb5` | Direct login form on Workbench |
| **OAuth2/OpenID Connect (OIDC)** | Using cloud IdP (Microsoft Entra ID, Okta, etc.) | [OAuth2/OIDC](https://docs.posit.co/ide/server-pro/authenticating-users.html#openid-connect-authentication) | SSO via external provider|
| **SAML** | Using enterprise SSO (Okta, Microsoft, others) | [SAML](https://docs.posit.co/ide/server-pro/authenticating-users.html#saml-single-sign-on-authentication) | SSO via external provider |
| **Proxied Authentication** | Authentication handled by reverse proxy (Shibboleth, Kerberos) | [Proxied Authentication](proxied_authentication.qmd) | Workbench trusts the proxy's auth header |

## PAM sessions 

Even when PAM is not used for authentication (e.g., with SAML or OIDC instead), PAM sessions are still used for home directory access, access controls, and user permissions. A PAM session is a server-side session started on behalf of a user, similar to what a remote shell would do. [PAM Sessions](https://docs.posit.co/ide/server-pro/admin/access_and_security/pam_sessions.html)]

The steps described in [Configuration for user provisioning](https://docs.posit.co/ide/server-pro/admin/user_provisioning/configuration.html) describe how to set this up. 

## User provisioning 

You must choose how to create (or verify) system accounts. Here's a quick comparison:

| Method | When to use | How it works | Supports groups? | Setup |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Manual account creation** | Small deployments or strict IT control | IT administrators create accounts with `useradd` before users sign in | ✅ Yes (via local `/etc/group` or NSS) | Create accounts: `sudo useradd -m username` |
| **LDAP/Active Directory** | On-premises directory service already exists | SSSD connects to LDAP/AD; PAM creates accounts on first login via `pam_mkhomedir` | ✅ Yes (groups from LDAP/AD) | [SSSD configuration](https://docs.posit.co/ide/server-pro/admin/user_provisioning/configuration.html) + `pam_mkhomedir` |
| **Just-in-Time (JIT)** | Using OIDC/SAML with simple provisioning | Workbench creates accounts automatically on first login based on identity provider claims | ❌ No | Enable: `user-provisioning-register-on-first-login=1` |
| **SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management)** | Using Okta or Microsoft Entra ID for full account/group sync | IdP sends account and group data to Workbench via SCIM API; groups synced automatically | ✅ Yes (groups from IdP) | [SCIM provisioning](https://docs.posit.co/ide/server-pro/admin/user_provisioning/user_provisioning.html) |
